figures.gloss: The primordial goddess of night, from whose immense darkness all creation emerged. Sensing the void, she divided herself into absolute light and shadow, and through a cunning deception, seduced her radiant brother Lucifer to conceive the first witch.
figures.facet: Unlike her chaste classical namesake, this Diana is a primordial creator and the explicitly subversive patron of the oppressed, her nocturnal power weaponized against feudal and Christian dominance.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899).

figures.gloss: The blindingly beautiful god of the sun and intellect. Ejected from the heavens for his unyielding pride, he fell into the earthly realm, bringing with him the illuminating spark of forbidden knowledge and magical sovereignty.
figures.facet: Completely divorced from the pure malevolence of Christian theology, he is celebrated as a Promethean light-bringer whose 'fall' catalyzed human intellectual and magical liberation.

figures.gloss: The holy witch-messiah, born of the union between Diana's darkness and Lucifer's light. She was dispatched to Earth with a militant gospel: to instruct the enslaved peasantry in the arts of cursing, herb-lore, and poison.
figures.facet: A radical, venomous inversion of the Christian savior. She preaches salvation not through turning the other cheek, but via active, magical sabotage and vengeance against cruel lords and priests.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Aradia' (1899); Raven Grimassi, 'Italian Witchcraft' (2000).

figures.gloss: The biblical pariah, reimagined by Italian folklore as the eternal 'Man in the Moon.' He is an astral wanderer trapped in the lunar sphere, endlessly carrying his bundle of thorns as a testament to his ancient exile.
figures.facet: Rather than a mere moral caution, he is embraced as the patron saint of vagabonds and exiles, transmuting his divine curse into a symbol of marginalized endurance.

figures.gloss: A nocturnal sovereign of the spirit realm, evolved from the biblical Herodias into a mythic queen. She rides the night winds alongside Diana, leading the spectral procession of souls and ecstatic dreamers to the Sabbat.
figures.facet: A pure product of medieval syncretism, she strips a New Testament villainess of her sin, elevating her to a psychopompic goddess of untrammeled female sovereignty and nocturnal flight.
figures.provenance: Carlo Ginzburg, 'Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath' (1989); Charles G. Leland (1899).

figures.gloss: The ancient ancestral spirits, often described as the 'Fatae' or the Old Ones. Originating in pre-Etruscan nature cults, they are venerated as the shadow-tutors who preserve the hidden rites of the Vecchia Religione across the centuries.
figures.facet: They provide a direct, animistic conduit to pre-Christian antiquity, functioning not as distant deities, but as intimate, demanding ancestors who enforce magical lineage.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition' (1892).

figures.gloss: The ecstatic seasonal gathering of the witch cult, a Sabbat marked by wild feasting, ritual nudity, and uninhibited dancing under the moon to honor Diana and commune with the unseen world.
figures.facet: It consciously reclaims the horrific, demonic Sabbat of the Inquisitorial imagination, transforming it into a joyous, anti-authoritarian festival that weaponizes flesh and pleasure against ascetic dogma.

figures.gloss: The dreaded Evil Eye; an invisible, destructive force of energetic contagion born from extreme envy or jealousy. It can wither crops, sicken livestock, and bring sudden misfortune, often cast entirely without the sender's conscious volition.
figures.facet: It reduces the complexities of magical warfare to a raw, involuntary discharge of social friction, requiring physical amulets like the cornicello to deflect the lethal gaze of neighbors.
figures.provenance: Widespread Italian folklore; Clarence Maloney (ed.), 'The Evil Eye' (1976).

figures.gloss: American folklorist (1824-1903) who collected Italian and Tuscan witch-lore in the 1890s and published 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899) and 'Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition' (1892), the two books that constitute nearly the entire textual base of Stregheria.
figures.facet: The tradition's actual point of origin, hiding in plain sight: every deity and rite the roster describes is filtered through his editorial hand, yet he is cited constantly in provenance while appearing nowhere as a figure.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899); 'Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition' (1892).

figures.gloss: Leland's Florentine fortune-teller informant, said to have collected material from Tuscan streghe and to have written out, partly in her own hand, the manuscript ('Vangelo') he translated into 'Aradia'.
figures.facet: The tradition's chain of transmission runs through one disputed woman: her existence in Leland's own account is documented, while the manuscript's authenticity as a pre-existing 'gospel' rather than her (or his) composition remains the open scholarly question the whole tradition rests on.
figures.provenance: Leland's own account in the preface and notes to 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899); discussed in Robert Mathiesen's introduction to the 1998 critical edition.

figures.gloss: Italian-American author (1951-2019) whose 'Ways of the Strega' (1995) and 'Italian Witchcraft' built the modern Aridian revival that gave the word 'Stregheria' its current currency, recasting Leland's Aradia material as a garbled survival of an organized old religion.
figures.facet: The word 'Stregheria' as a modern tradition-name is largely his: where Leland recorded folklore and Ginzburg analyzed trial records, Grimassi is the one who assembled both into a practiced revival religion with its own liturgical calendar and god-names.
figures.provenance: Raven Grimassi, 'Ways of the Strega' (1995); 'Italian Witchcraft: The Old Religion of Southern Italy' (2000).

figures.gloss: Leland's 1899 publication of an alleged witches' gospel: Diana's cosmogony and union with Lucifer, Aradia's mission to the oppressed, and a collection of charms, invocations, and instructions for the Vecchia Religione's rites.
figures.facet: The single 'canonical' document behind Diana, Lucifer, Aradia, and Cain as roster entries — its contested status (genuine folk gospel, informant composition, or Leland's own literary construction) is itself the central scholarly question about Stregheria.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899); critical edition and introduction by Robert Mathiesen and Chas S. Clifton (1998).

figures.gloss: The star-goddess name Leland's Tuscan informants gave as the old name of Diana; Grimassi's Aridian Stregheria revived Tana (paired with a consort, Tanus) as the movement's own cult-names for the Great Goddess and God.
figures.facet: Documents the revival stratum by its own preferred vocabulary: where the Vangelo strata speaks of Diana and Lucifer, the 1990s revival speaks of Tana and Tanus — the same divine pairing carried under the name its living practitioners actually use.
figures.provenance: Charles G. Leland, 'Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition' (1892); Raven Grimassi, 'Ways of the Strega' (1995).

figures.gloss: The Neapolitan sprig-of-rue silver charm, its branches hung with moon, key, serpent, vervain-flower and other apotropaic tokens, worn as a protective amulet against the evil eye and general misfortune.
figures.facet: The tradition's signature material object: where the malocchio entry names the curse, the cimaruta is the physical counter-technology, documented in 19th-century folklore collection independent of Leland's texts.
figures.provenance: Frederick Thomas Elworthy, 'The Evil Eye: An Account of This Ancient and Widespread Superstition' (1895).

figures.gloss: The legendary 'noce di Benevento,' a walnut tree beneath which streghe were said to convene for their nocturnal gathering, recorded from the 17th century as the most famous single site of Italian witch-lore.
figures.facet: Gives the Treguenda entry's implied Sabbat a named, specific place: the earliest strand of the tradition's substrate, predating both Leland's text and Grimassi's revival by over two centuries.
figures.provenance: Pietro Piperno, 'De nuce maga beneventana' (1639); pervasive in southern Italian folklore collections through the 20th century.

figures.gloss: Friulian 'good walkers,' known from Inquisition trial records (1575-1675), who claimed to leave their bodies in ecstatic trance at the Ember Days to fight witches, armed with fennel stalks, for the fertility of the year's harvest.
figures.facet: Carlo Ginzburg's case study behind the roster's Erodiade and Treguenda framing — added here with the honest caveat that the Benandanti are a documented Friulian phenomenon, geographically and institutionally distinct from Stregheria proper, used only as comparative evidence for an older ecstatic substrate.
figures.provenance: Carlo Ginzburg, 'The Night Battles: Witchcraft and Agrarian Cults in the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Centuries' (1966; Eng. tr. 1983); 'Ecstasies: Deciphering the Witches' Sabbath' (1989).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The primordial division of the cosmos into dark and light, bound by an incestuous desire that births magic.
Intra-tradition bond: The divine mother and her messianic daughter, cooperating to bring the old religion to the oppressed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Aradia actively mirrors Christ's mission but substitutes meekness and forgiveness with vengeance, poison, and peasant rebellion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The defiant, radiant figure who disobeys supreme authority to bring the fire of forbidden knowledge to humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A cosmological structure where light and dark are absolute, necessary halves of the divine whole, rather than moral poles of good versus evil.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Leland's Diana explicitly draws upon and syncretizes the classical Roman/Greek goddess, repurposing her entirely as the subversive Queen of Witches.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A mythic figure permanently exiled to the moon, whose lunar presence is the direct, visible result of a violent primordial transgression.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ecstatic, communal night-dance that serves as the primary engine for spiritual communion, physical healing, and social release.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine emissary who miraculously appears to the earthly populace specifically to impart the secret, practical arts of magic and divination.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A formidable, elemental female sovereign who presides over ecstatic, nocturnal gatherings of witches and spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A volatile, visceral power arising from social friction or envy that must be carefully managed or deflected with physical wards.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cursed biblical wanderer is elevated by the witches into a figure of sympathy, entirely rejecting Yahweh's original punitive judgment.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The spontaneous splitting of a primordial cosmic whole into an active light principle and a receptive dark principle, required to generate the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The active, venerated shades of the ancestors who are directly invoked to guide the living in spiritual and daily matters.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Leland traces the Lasa directly to Etruscan underworld deities, viewing them as a surviving, analogical continuation of ancient animistic entities.

Cross-tradition: two regional traditions of an alleged witches' nocturnal assembly, recorded through hostile or reconstructive external documentation rather than practitioners' own contemporaneous testimony.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Leland's 1899 Aradia gospel supplies a substantial portion of the actual wording Valiente wove into the Charge of the Goddess.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Aradia's 1899 charter myth — the witch as the Goddess's liberationist teacher to the oppressed poor — supplies the movement's origin story for witchcraft-as-goddess-religion, however contested Leland's actual source material.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the same biblical exile reread in two divergent registers — Stregheria's marked Man-in-the-Moon, Luciferianism's first initiate — both descend from Genesis 4's mark of Cain as banishment-into-otherness.

The modern Stregheria revival's Diana claims descent from this Roman goddess via Charles Leland's Aradia (1899) — a documented revival-of claim resting on a single contested folklore source, not proven cultic continuity.
Intra-tradition bond: the collector-translator and the text he published — direct authorship, not folklore filtered through generations.
Intra-tradition bond: the folklorist and his disputed informant, the two ends of the tradition's single chain of transmission.
Intra-tradition bond: the revival author and the cult-name his Aridian Stregheria uses for the Great Goddess.
Intra-tradition bond: Tana is Leland's own recorded old name for Diana, carried forward as the revival's preferred cult-name for the same goddess.
Intra-tradition bond: the text is the sole source that names and sends the messianic figure it is titled for.
Intra-tradition bond: the amulet and the curse it is worn to deflect, ward and threat from the same folk-magic register.
Intra-tradition bond: the legendary named site and the nocturnal gathering rite Italian folklore places beneath it.
Structural parallel only, not descent: Ginzburg reads both as evidence of an older ecstatic fertility-cult substrate, though the Benandanti are Friulian trial-record material, not Stregheria proper.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: ecstatic soul-travel undertaken on the community's behalf, the body left behind while the spirit contests unseen forces for the group's fortune.

Valiente's Charge of the Goddess (1950s-57 verse revision of Gardner's draft) draws directly on Leland's 1899 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches,' whose messianic Aradia is Stregheria's central savior-figure — in Wiccan liturgy the name survives as a borrowed invocation, not a worshipped person.
Same name, opposite valence: Christianity's fallen adversary becomes, in Stregheria, Diana's radiant brother-consort, the sun itself.

dossier.essence: A modern form of 'Italian witchcraft' whose textual root is Charles G. Leland's 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899), which claimed to record a surviving pagan witch-religion of the Tuscan peasantry centered on the goddess Diana, her brother-consort Lucifer, and their daughter, the witch-messiah Aradia. Revived and reworked in late-twentieth-century America — notably by Raven Grimassi — it presents itself as 'la Vecchia Religione,' the Old Religion of Italy, though its claim to unbroken pagan descent is disputed by scholars.
dossier.cosmos: In Leland's gospel Diana is the primal goddess of night from whom all creation springs; dividing herself into darkness and light, she seduces the light-bearer Lucifer and bears Aradia, who is sent to earth to teach the oppressed the arts of witchcraft against their lords and priests. Modern Stregheria elaborates this into a broadly animistic, immanent nature-religion with ancestral spirits (the Lasa, or Fatae) and lunar and seasonal powers. Belief in the malocchio, the evil eye, and its remedies is continuous with genuine Italian folk practice.
dossier.practice: Contemporary practitioners keep seasonal Sabbats (the treguenda), honor the goddess and the ancestral Old Ones, and work folk-magical and protective rites — much of it continuous with real Italian and Italian-American custom, from charms against the evil eye and the cornicello horn to herb- and saint-lore. Groups are typically small and initiatory, in the manner of modern Pagan witchcraft.
dossier.canon: Charles G. Leland's 'Aradia, or the Gospel of the Witches' (1899) and his 'Etruscan Roman Remains in Popular Tradition' (1892); in the revival, the books of Raven Grimassi, such as 'Italian Witchcraft' (2000).
dossier.reflects: A modern religion assembled from a contested folklore text — pagan survival asserted as continuous lineage, and witchcraft reimagined as the sacred resistance of the oppressed.
sources: Sabina Magliocco, 'Witching Culture: Folklore and Neo-Paganism in America' (2004)
Ronald Hutton, 'The Triumph of the Moon: A History of Modern Pagan Witchcraft' (1999)
Sabina Magliocco, 'Who Was Aradia? The History and Development of a Legend' (2002)
